title: How AI Sees: Pictures Come Alive

modules.title: What Does AI Actually See?
modules.sub: Discover that AI sees numbers, not pictures — yet.
modules.description: Learners enter a laboratory story where a young scientist named Zara shows them that to a computer, a photo of a cat is really just a giant grid of numbers. Through a playful pixel-colouring activity, learners 'become' the computer and try to recognise shapes from grids alone. This builds the foundational insight that AI pattern recognition is mathematical, not magical.

modules.title: Teaching AI to Spot Things
modules.sub: See how thousands of labelled examples train an AI's eye.
modules.description: Zara's AI assistant can't tell a dog from a muffin — a real and famously funny AI problem. Learners play the role of human labellers, tagging images to teach the AI, then discover how getting labels wrong creates a confused AI. This module makes the concept of supervised learning tangible and funny before it becomes technical.

modules.title: When AI Eyes Get It Wrong
modules.sub: Explore surprising mistakes AI image systems make.
modules.description: Learners are presented with real categories of AI vision errors — objects misidentified because of lighting, angle, or things the AI never saw in training. They choose which errors matter most and why, building early intuition about consequences: a self-driving car misreading a stop sign is very different from a photo app mistagging a holiday album. The module closes with a learner-ranked list of 'AI vision mistakes I'd least want to happen.'

modules.title: Build Your Own Image Sorter
modules.sub: Create a simple AI that sorts pictures into your categories.
modules.description: Using a no-code visual training tool, learners collect ten images each of two categories they choose — real-world objects they can photograph or find — and train a mini classifier. They test it, celebrate what it gets right, and investigate what confuses it. The trained model card, explaining what it can and cannot do, is the exit artifact.

synopsis: A playful investigation into how AI turns pixels into meaning — and why it sometimes gets things hilariously wrong. Young learners explore computer vision by doing it themselves, hands-on and step by step.
rationale: Image recognition is one of the most visible AI systems children encounter daily — in games, apps, and cameras — yet almost no child-facing education explains how it actually works at a conceptual level.
learning_outcome: Students can explain that AI recognises images through patterns in numerical data, describe how labelled training examples teach an AI, and build and test a simple image classifier of their own design.
source_signals: Project Overview ‹ PopBots: An early childhood AI curriculum - MIT Media Lab
